text:
The fuel oil market is notoriously difficult and opaque. Lots of guesswork involved and figuring out what component (even crude!) is making sense into the blend pool. 

I'm going to break it all down for you with the help of my esteemed colleagues Hoa and Jay. 

Join us for the webinar for a Singapore-friendly hour on Tuesday 4th August.

Link to register below.

#oott

Russia is struggling to sell its crude oil, with floating inventories building up again. Its troubles are compounded by the need to sell overseas a growing amount of barrels as Ukrainian attacks have reduced its domestic refining capacity. 

https://t.co/GJgaEpBr5K

3-2-1 crack spread just hit $68/bbl. 

Congrats Bessent and BOJ 👏

You guys really fucked it up. Now if crude rallies, refining margins won’t fall. You hit the wall sooner and faster. https://t.co/FvtcMTjPXR

Strait of Hormuz over the last 48 hours: Back to the start.

As the war rages on, Fujairah as the main tanker shuttle port is all but dead after the IRGC fired missiles at two VLCCs, the first escalation of its type yesterday.

Even with US protection, it was not enough, and this is going to take ~6 million b/d of flow out of the market.

To further add to my point that the escalation is increasing, this segment from the WSJ article is very concerning:

"There was also concern among shippers that the scope of Iran’s attacks was widening away from the immediate vicinity of the strait after a vessel was targeted off Oman early Tuesday, according to its owner, London-based Stolt-Nielsen. It had not crossed the strait at all and was loaded with Omani chemicals headed to Malaysia, according to Kpler."

We are in no-man's land now. Either Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz or it doesn't. It's all-or-nothing.

I have included the video from @MarineTraffic.

Fujairah is done.

"Sinokor and MSC executives are concerned the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps will continue to attack their ships to try to shut down the shuttle market, said people familiar with the matter. At least two captains on their tankers have refused to go through the strait in recent days."

@JuliOnTwtr

A reminder that the real stress in energy markets is not in crude oil but in refined products. While Brent has rallied back above USD 80, diesel, gasoil and jet fuel continue to command exceptionally strong premiums as low inventories, refinery disruptions and peak seasonal demand tighten product availability.

As the charts highlight, crack spreads across the US, Europe and Asia have surged to multi-year highs, signalling that refiners - not crude producers - are currently capturing the bulk of the value. Refining margins are now approaching historic levels, creating a major windfall for operators at a time when global middle-distillate inventories remain critically low and supply chains continue to face disruption. Until refined product stocks begin to rebuild or demand eases seasonally, these elevated margins are likely to keep the energy complex well supported, even if crude supply itself remains relatively comfortable.

#Commodities:

#CrudeOil prices trade higher for a third day after Trump threatened further strikes on Iran, hours after the US resumed its blockade on Iran’s shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Meanwhile, Trump’s U-turn on plans to impose a 20% charge on shipments through the waterway briefly sent prices lower before the resumption of attacks, including Houthi groups firing ballistic missiles and drones on Saudi Arabia added to concerns about supply from the region. Focus on US stockpiles amid the release of weekly data from the EIA.

#Gold rallied to USD 4,100 after softer-than-expected US CPI data raised expectations for a less aggressive Federal Reserve. However, the move quickly reversed, with prices slipping back toward USD 4,030 after Fed Chair Kevin Warsh signalled monetary policy may still need to be tightened. At the same time, renewed gains in oil prices and fresh US strikes against Iran revived inflation concerns, shifting market focus back toward the risk of higher interest rates rather than the longer-term, gold-supportive economic fallout from a sustained energy shock. For now, gold is sending no clear directional signal, with the yellow metal continuing to consolidate following a prolonged and deep correction.
